project_overview.project_development_objective: To reduce vulnerability to hydrometeorological hazards and improve living conditions in selected low-income areas in participating municipalities.
project_overview.total_financing: US$70 million (two IDA credits: US$50 million and US$20 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: 167,228 people in La Paz (50,080) and Santa Cruz de la Sierra (117,148). Indirect beneficiaries: all residents of both municipalities. Municipal staff from both cities will benefit from training and capacity building.

primary_locations: La Paz (macrodistrict of La Portada, Irpavi and Orkojahuira river valleys, Kantutani area, Cotahuma River)
Santa Cruz de la Sierra (Eighth Ring Drainage Channel area, Pirai River area, City Center, southwest part along South-West 8th ring road)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Bolivia is experiencing political transition after contested October 2019 elections with new elections scheduled for May 3, 2020. However, the project areas are not in active conflict zones but are vulnerable to natural hazards and characterized by low-income informal settlements.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Resilient Infrastructure and Urban Upgrading in Santa Cruz de la Sierra
key_components.description: Sub-component 1.1 (US$34.7M): Construction of 6.4 km drainage canal, Pirai Metropolitan River Park (4.2 km), flood early warning system, and capacity building. Sub-component 1.2 (US$11.4M): Urban neighborhood upgrading in City Center, 65 km of bicycle lanes, non-motorized transport masterplan.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Resilient Infrastructure, Slope Stabilization and Urban Upgrading in La Paz
key_components.description: Sub-component 2.1 (US$11.3M): River channeling along Irpavi and Orkojahuira rivers, slope stabilization works, Cotahuma river channeling. Sub-component 2.2 (US$8.0M): Upgrading of 9 neighborhoods with street improvements, basic services, street lighting, recreational parks, community centers.
Project audits, monitoring and evaluation, citizen engagement, public consultations, communication campaigns. Includes support for Project Coordination Units in both municipalities.
key_components.financing: US$4.5 million (US$3.8M Santa Cruz, US$0.7M La Paz)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Autonomous Municipal Governments: Municipality of Santa Cruz de la Sierra (GAMSC) through Municipal Secretariat of Administration and Finance (SMAF), and Municipality of La Paz (GAMLP) through Municipal Secretariat of Integral Risk Management (SMGIR). Each will establish a Project Implementation Unit (PIU).
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Units in each municipality with dedicated staff for project management, technical oversight, and coordination. Coordination with Ministry of Public Works, Services and Housing (MOPSV) for alignment with national urban policy. Coordination with development partners including IADB, CAF, JICA, UN Habitat, ILO, and Swiss SECO for parallel grant financing.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Open international competitive bidding for contracts ≥US$6.7M; open national competitive bidding for US$250K-US$6.7M; request for quotations for <US$250K. Quality and Cost-Based Selection for consulting services. Use of STEP system for procurement tracking.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Project monitoring system established by PIUs to track progress toward results indicators. Semi-annual Interim Financial Reports, annual Project financial statements with independent audit. Monitoring of PDO indicators including households benefiting from flood protection, people with improved living conditions, public space improvements. Grievance Redress Mechanism with 21-day response target. Mid-term review at year 3. Bank supervision missions twice per year.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from ongoing political tensions after disputed October 2019 elections, upcoming general elections in May 2020, and potential municipal elections that may result in changes in local administrations. Increased complexity of stakeholder interests and positions due to political changes.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Confirmed interest and commitment of transitional government to Project under same terms. Close and continuous coordination with central and subnational governments throughout transitions. Technical assistance to support municipal governments in developing implementation capacity. Selection of priority 'no regrets' investments included in urban development strategies with wide support among different parties and communities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from large macroeconomic imbalances (fiscal deficit 7.8% of GDP, current account deficit 5.1% of GDP in 2019) that might affect resources available for infrastructure construction, operation and maintenance. Public debt increased to 57% of GDP, international reserves dropped to 15%, public savings fell to 12%.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Full financing of civil works from credit proceeds. Infrastructure designs using techniques to reduce maintenance needs. Strengthening of local operations and maintenance capacities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from absence of urban development policy at national level. While 2014 Risk Management Law (Law No. 602) establishes DRM framework, it needs adaptation to urban planning and growth context.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Maintain dialogue with government on urban resilience during implementation. Coordination with MOPSV which is developing cities policy including urban resilience and climate change pillar. SECO grant expected to contribute to future national urban development policy.

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from multi-sectoral nature of project (mobility, urban development, disaster risk management) which is not common practice for investment projects in Bolivia. Complexity of coordinating different municipal departments.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Municipalities have developed coordination mechanisms at local level with clear roles and responsibilities. Technical assistance provided by World Bank. Bank review of final designs for works >US$2M based on complexity and risk.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from limited municipal capacity for operations and maintenance of assets. Multiple sectoral departments involved. GAMSC has no prior World Bank project experience.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close coordination and communication. Technical assistance to strengthen capacity for disaster risk management, municipal management, and operations and maintenance. Both municipalities have adequate capacity within respective Project Implementing Entities (SMAF and SMGIR). Design of infrastructure to be more resilient and require less frequent maintenance. Development of O&M strategies and training for municipal staff.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from: (i) high staff turnover accentuated by upcoming municipal elections; (ii) complexity of monitoring and controlling civil works in financial terms; (iii) potential delays in budget reformulation due to complex administrative procedures; (iv) GAMSC PIU lacks World Bank project experience and knowledge of Bank Procurement and FM Regulations; (v) challenge of hiring and maintaining qualified staff.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Terms of reference for fiduciary staff approved by Bank prior to implementation. Project Procurement Strategy for Development. Implementation support and training on Procurement and FM Regulations. Detailed Project Operational Manual with fiduciary arrangements. Regular Bank supervision visits and annual Procurement Post Review. Close follow-up with government entities for timely budget recording. Use of SIGEP/SIAP system and Single Treasury Account for financial management.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from limited institutional capacity to implement environmental and social safeguards in both municipalities. Potential adverse social impacts from resettlement, labor influx, and community relations.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Clear definition of roles and responsibilities for safeguards implementation. Staff training and access to additional support. Preparation of Environmental Impact Assessment and Environmental Management Plans. Environmental Management Frameworks for future subprojects. Resettlement Action Plans and Resettlement Policy Frameworks. Social Management Frameworks with community relations systems, communication strategies, and grievance redress mechanisms. Gender-sensitive participatory planning. Standard measures in bid documents to mitigate labor influx impacts including Contractor's Personnel Code of Conduct.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risk from multiple communities in Project areas with disparate views on urban resilience issues. Many development partners working on various sectoral issues requiring coordination to minimize duplication.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Promotion and support of stakeholder engagement efforts. Community-based approach to design and implementation. Coordination with development partners (IADB, CAF, JICA, UN Habitat, ILO, SECO). Citizen engagement strategy with two-way communication. Grievance Redress Mechanism.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level Grievance Redress Mechanisms (GRM) established in both municipalities with 21-day response target for addressing grievances. Communities can also submit complaints to World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) or Inspection Panel. GRM indicators tracked in results framework with target of 75% of grievances addressed according to service standards.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project contributes to World Bank Gender Equality Strategy (2016-2023). Addresses low female representation and decision-making at local level. Gender Analysis conducted in March 2018. Targeted focus groups divided by gender and generation to identify needs and priorities. Gender-specific participatory planning activities and consultations. Indicator on women's needs reflected in project design measured by concrete proposals from women at community participation events. Target of 90,113 women among 167,228 people provided with improved urban living conditions.
vulnerable_groups: Low-income households (32% of population in target municipalities, over 900,000 people)
Residents of informal settlements on steep slopes and floodplains
Households affected by natural disasters (9% suffered disaster in previous 12 months, 50% suffered material losses)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is not in active conflict zone but operates in context of political instability. Bolivia is experiencing political transition after contested October 2019 elections with civil unrest and resignation of president. Transitional government installed November 12, 2019 with new elections scheduled May 3, 2020. Project addresses underlying vulnerabilities of poor populations to natural hazards which can exacerbate social tensions. Project design includes community engagement and participatory approaches to build social cohesion. Investments are 'no regrets' priorities with wide support among different political parties and communities.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Bolivia faces multiple fragility factors: (i) Political fragility - contested elections, political transition, upcoming elections creating uncertainty; (ii) Economic fragility - macroeconomic imbalances (fiscal deficit 7.8% GDP, current account deficit 5.1% GDP), declining commodity prices, reduced growth from 6.8% (2013) to 2.2% (2019), poverty reduction stalled; (iii) Environmental fragility - high vulnerability to climate change and natural disasters, 20% of population in areas at risk to 3+ hazards, 21% of GDP generated in hazard-prone areas; (iv) Social fragility - rapid urbanization creating informal settlements, low-income populations with limited access to services, high poverty rates (32% in target municipalities), limited institutional capacity at municipal level.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project areas in La Paz and Santa Cruz de la Sierra are not in active conflict zones. However, security considerations include: (i) labor influx risks from construction works - mitigated through Contractor's Personnel Code of Conduct linked to Bolivian Law 328 to prevent gender-based violence; (ii) community tensions from resettlement - addressed through Resettlement Action Plans and Resettlement Policy Frameworks with consultation and compensation; (iii) informal settlement risks in stabilized areas - managed through Municipal Law No 233 'Ley de Fiscalizacion y Control Territorial'; (iv) staff security during implementation given political instability.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Involuntary Resettlement OP/BP 4.12 triggered. Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) prepared for drainage subproject in Santa Cruz de la Sierra with public consultations October 5-16, 2018 (22 neighborhood representatives, 32% women). Resettlement Policy Frameworks prepared for both La Paz and Santa Cruz de la Sierra. For La Paz landslide subproject, verified that stabilized area will not require resettlement and will not affect current resettlement programs for families affected by April 2019 landslide (64 buildings collapsed, 251 households affected, 380 people left homeless). Stabilized area will not be used for housing in future. Risk of informal settlement in stabilized area managed through Municipal Law No 233.
